question_text: A rod's length is reported as 200 cm with an absolute uncertainty of ±2 cm. What is the percentage uncertainty (percentage error) in this measurement?
explanation: Percentage (or relative) uncertainty is defined as (absolute uncertainty ÷ measured value) × 100%. Here absolute uncertainty = 2 cm and measured value = 200 cm. Thus percentage uncertainty = (2/200)×100% = 1%. Option B (0.1%) corresponds to incorrectly dividing by 2000 instead of 200. Option C (2%) would correspond to taking the uncertainty as 4 cm or misplacing the decimal. Option D (10%) is an order-of-magnitude error. Units cancel in the ratio, so the result is dimensionless and expressed as a percent.
more_explanation: Absolute uncertainty gives the possible variation in the measured quantity (±2 cm). Relative uncertainty is this variation compared to the size of the quantity and is useful to compare precision across different magnitudes. Here the relative uncertainty is 0.01 (1%). In practice, percentage uncertainty helps judge whether the measurement precision is adequate for the intended purpose.
concept_tested: Absolute and percentage (relative) error in measurements
